codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of the Football tab feature. The page at app/football/page.tsx correctly implements all required components: search bar, World Cup banner, section header with Trophy icon, and match cards with country flags flanking the VS text. The API endpoint at app/api/events/route.ts properly supports type filtering and search query parameters. The MatchCard component has a clean, distinct visual style with flags on left/right sides of 'VS' text, formatted date/time, venue with pin emoji, and price in $XX.XX format. Tests are comprehensive (7 API + 9 component). Two non-blocking issues noted: (1) use of `any` type for the Prisma where clause, and (2) the football page doesn't check response.ok before parsing the fetch response, meaning a server error response would silently fail to show data rather than displaying an error state. Both are minor.

issues.description: Uses `const where: any = {}` instead of a typed Prisma where clause. With TypeScript strict mode enabled, this bypasses type safety. Could use `Prisma.EventWhereInput` from '@prisma/client' for proper typing. This follows the same pattern as the products route, so it's a pre-existing convention rather than a new deviation.

The fetch call doesn't check `response.ok` before calling `response.json()`. If the API returns a 500 error with `{ error: 'Failed to fetch events' }`, the page would silently treat the error object as the events array, resulting in no cards displayed but no error feedback. Should check response.ok and handle error state gracefully.

Client-side search filtering duplicates logic already available in the API's `?q=` parameter. The page fetches all football events once, then filters locally. This is fine for the current small dataset but could be a concern at scale. The approach is consistent with how the feature was specified (real-time filtering as user types), so it's an acceptable architecture choice for now.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The API route uses `const where: any = {}` for building Prisma query filters. The products route (app/api/products/route.ts) uses a similar untyped approach. Neither AGENTS.md nor architecture.md documents a convention for Prisma query typing. This is a minor consistency issue that could be documented if the team wants to enforce typed Prisma inputs.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/api/events/route.ts:11 `const where: any = {}` — same pattern as app/api/products/route.ts which avoids explicit Prisma types entirely

sharedStateObservations.observation: Neither the events route nor the products route check for response.ok on the client side before parsing JSON. This is an emerging pattern where API error responses are silently ignored. Could be worth documenting the expected client-side error handling pattern in AGENTS.md.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/football/page.tsx:34 — `const data = await response.json()` without checking response.ok. Same pattern exists in other tab pages.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The next.config.ts was updated to allow picsum.photos and i.pravatar.cc image domains. This is a shared configuration change that affects all features using Next.js Image component with external URLs. The architecture.md could note which image domains are configured for seed data.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: next.config.ts remotePatterns now includes picsum.photos and i.pravatar.cc — needed for seed data images across multiple features

summary: Football tab implementation passes review. All required components are present: search bar with 'Find matches' placeholder, World Cup banner image, Football section header with Trophy icon, and match cards with country flags on left/right sides of VS text. The GET /api/events endpoint correctly supports type filtering and search. Tests provide good coverage of API and component behavior. Two non-blocking issues: untyped Prisma where clause and missing response.ok check on fetch. The implementation follows established project conventions and the worker followed the skill procedure correctly.
